observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the OIC, General Services Officer and the OIC, Municipal Accountant to properly plan and conduct the physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) and ensure timely and complete submission of RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor; and Instruct the OIC, General Services Officer and the OIC, Municipal Accountant to work together in reconciling and updating both their records. Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments on any discrepancies noted to present fairly the balances in the financial statements; Likewise, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to finally establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.
observations.managementAction: The management established an inventory system, conversely, a discrepancy of ₱123.953 million exist between the PPE balances of the General Services Office and the Accounting Unit. The Inventory Committee was not able to conduct the physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment for CY 2024.
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee was not able to conduct the physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment for CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the OIC, Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to locate necessary documents supporting the dormant items composing the CIP account so that proper adjustments could be effected in the books to reflect the correct balance of the account.
observations.managementAction: The reconciliation process is being conducted by the management, considering the unavailability of the required documents and the status of the dormant items composing the CIP remain unavailable, thus, adjustments could not be made in the books to reflect the correct balance of the account.
observations.reason: The request for retrieving the necessary documents is on-going; unavailability of supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to officially transfer the above Property, Plant and Equipment to the Department of Education, by securing authority from the Sangguniang Bayan by way of resolution, to authorize the Municipal Mayor to execute a Deed of Donation or a Memorandum of Agreement. A copy of the journal entry voucher (JEV) prepared by the Department of Education recording the receipt of properties transferred by the LGU should also be submitted to COA.
observations.managementAction: The management was not able to transfer the PPEs to the Department due to the unavailability of the necessary documents and reconciliation.
observations.reason: Unavailability of the necessary documents and reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require: a. The OIC-Municipal Accountant to cause the preparation of adjusting entries of reconciling items with available documentation or reference; and b. The OIC, Municipal Accountant to summarize all reconciling items without available supporting documents and references and submit the same with a query to the COA Government Accountancy Sector (GAS) for its appropriate accounting treatment and remedies to clean the LGU’s books.
observations.managementAction: The management is conducting on-going reconciliation of the accounts, thus several reconciling items with supporting documents remained unadjusted in the agency’s books.
observations.reason: On-going reconciliation of the accounts; several reconciling items with supporting documents remained unadjusted in the agency’s books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to identify the official/s accountable for the incurrence of bank charges totaling ₱38,734.40, and cause the refund thereof.
observations.managementAction: The request for the closure of the account is currently being processed by management; no refund was made by the accountable officer/s as of reporting.
observations.reason: Request for closure of the PNB account is being processed; no refund made by the accountable officer/s.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the: a. Inventory Committee to properly plan and conduct the physical count of all PPEs and ensure timely and complete submission of RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor; and b. OIC, General Services Officer and the OIC, Municipal Accountant to work together in reconciling and updating both their records. Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments on any discrepancies noted. Likewise, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to finally establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee was not able to conduct the physical count of Property, Plant and Equipment for CY 2024. The PPE balances of the General Services Office and the Accounting Unit remained unreconciled. The Inventory Committee conducted partial inventory in CY 2023. The Management established an inventory system; however, reconciliation remained incomplete.
observations.reason: Physical count for CY 2024 was not conducted; reconciliation remained incomplete.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to: a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to secure the approval of the BLGF so that the LGU can continue to maintain its existing current account with the PNB; and b. Instruct the OIC, Municipal Accountant to summarize all reconciling items without available supporting documents and references, and submit the same with a query to the COA Government Accountancy Sector (GAS) for its appropriate accounting treatment and remedies to clean the LGU’s books.
observations.managementAction: Management coordinated and is conducting ongoing reconciliation of reconciling items. No refund was made by the accountable officer/s for previous bank charges.
observations.reason: On-going reconciliation; no refund made by the accountable officer/s.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to: a. Conduct validation of the Unexpended balances as of December 31, 2022 and determine if there are concerned Source Agencies with valid claims; Return/remit to Source Agencies any unexpended amount of fund transfer after project completion pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994; and b. Reconcile with Source Agencies unused fund transfers, prepare adjusting entry for reconciling items and initiate the filing of write-off for undocumented dormant accounts taking into consideration the requirements as provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: Partial reconciliation was conducted during CY 2024; management coordinated with source agencies and is awaiting further instructions.
observations.reason: Partial reconciliation was conducted; coordination with source agencies ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require: a. The Municipal Treasurer to request reconciling items documents from the banks to support the unrecorded deposits and debit and credit memoranda to enable the OIC, Municipal Accountant to cause the preparation of adjusting journal entries of reconciling items; b. The Municipal Treasurer and OIC, Municipal Accountant to undertake reconciliation of the cashbook balances with accounting records regularly in order for necessary corrections and adjustments to be taken up in the books in a timely manner; and c. Concerned officials and employees to secure a copy of the court decision or other official reports/document declaring the shortage of the former Municipal Treasurer in the amount of ₱673,440.11 to facilitate the recording and reclassification of the shortage.
observations.managementAction: Partial reconciliation was undertaken during 2024. No documents were available to facilitate recording and reclassification of the shortage. No refund was made by the accountable officer/s for bank service charges.
observations.reason: Partial reconciliation was undertaken during CY 2024; no documents were available to facilitate recording and reclassification of the shortage; no refund made by the accountable officer/s.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to: a. Direct the General Services Officer to identify the unserviceable properties subject to inspection and appraisal in order to expedite their disposal; and b. Direct the Disposal Committee to follow the duly procedures and accomplish the forms to be used in reporting disposable supplies and property.
observations.managementAction: Procedures were partially conducted during CY 2023; inventory of unserviceable properties was partially conducted. Disposal procedures were not fully followed.
observations.reason: Inventory of unserviceable properties was only partially conducted; disposal procedures were not fully followed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the: a. OIC-Municipal Accountant to review all outstanding dormant trust liabilities, and perform a reconciliation with the Source Agencies for the balances; and b. Initiate return/remittance of unexpended balances as appropriate.
observations.managementAction: Partial reconciliation was conducted during CY 2024; further reconciliation with source agencies is on-going.
observations.reason: Partial reconciliation conducted; reconciliation with source agencies on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to double its effort to collect the loan overdue balance and to strengthen the implementing guidelines and monitoring of the program.
observations.managementAction: Partial collection was undertaken; ₱2,000 was collected as evidenced by official receipt number 8927133 dated July 13, 2023.
observations.reason: Collection effort ongoing; only ₱2,000 collected as of July 13, 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Direct the Inventory Committee to properly plan and conduct
the physical count of all PPEs to establish the existence, validity,
and accuracy of PPEs and ensure timely and complete
submission of the RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor;
b. Instruct the OIC, Municipal Accountant, and GSO to prepare
their respective reports and work closely on the reconciliation
and updating of their records. Thereafter, make the necessary
adjustments on any discrepancies noted to present fairly the
balances in the financial statements; and
c. Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to review the
computations in the PPE Ledger Cards and make necessary
adjustments to accurately reflect the balances of the PPE
Accounts in the Statement of Financial Position, and recognize
the exact amount of Depreciation Expense in the Statement of
Financial Performance.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the OIC-Municipal
Accountant to:

a. Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
• Instruct the MTO to provide the RPT reports used for
receivable recognition and establish the receivables at the
start of the next year.;
• Perform the review of the Real Property Tax Receivables
account in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer to
determine the variances for corrections/adjustments;
• Conduct regular reconciliation of the Real Property Tax
Receivables balance with the MTO; and
• Exert effort to identify the discrepancies between the Real
Property Tax Receivables and its reciprocal account,
reconcile the same and make the necessary adjustments, as
warranted, to bring the accounts to their correct balances;

b. Require the Municipal Treasurer to:
• Submit the RPT reports being used for receivable
recognition to the OIC, Municipal Accountant in the first
week of the start of the year for the timely and proper
recognition of the Real Property Tax Receivables;
• Coordinate with the OIC-Municipal Accountant in the
review of the Real Property Tax Receivables account to
determine the variances for corrections/adjustments; and
• Conduct regular reconciliation of the Real Property Tax
Receivables balance with the OIC, Municipal Accountant; and

c. Instruct the Municipal Assessor to reconcile their records for
Real Property Tax with the records of the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to ensure the
accuracy and integrity of information across all responsible
units.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
a. Direct the Inventory Committee to:
• Conduct faithfully the physical count of inventories on a
semestral basis and prepare completely the RPCI for all
inventory accounts in the succeeding years;
• Ensure that reconciliation of all inventory accounts is
undertaken before the submission of the RPCI to the Audit
Team;
• Ensure to include in the Stock Cards, Semi-Expendable
Property Cards and RPCI essential information about the
inventories: for the semi-expendable property: the
property custodian, location and condition; and for drugs
and medicines, the expiry dates;
• Strictly comply with the set deadlines in the submission of
RPCI; and
• Facilitate the immediate tagging of all semi-expendable
property;
b. Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
• Reconcile their records with the those of the GSO and
MHO and identify the variances for proper disposition;
• Strictly apply the Perpetual Inventory Method in
recording the supplies and materials for stock;
• Prepare and maintain the ledgers for the semi-expendable
property;
• Prepare the journal entries for the dropping from the
books of the issued semi-expendable property; and
• Effect immediately the necessary adjustments or
corrections in the books to bring the inventory accounts to
its correct balance; and
c. Require the GSO-Head and Municipal Health Officer to:
• Prepare and regularly update the stock cards of the
inventories for the drugs and medicines to include vital
information; and
• Reconcile their records with the accounting records and
effect the necessary corrections in their stock cards.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the
OIC-Municipal Accountant to:

a. Analyze immediately the Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs
accounts and identify the true nature of the transactions
for the proper disposition of the deficiencies;
b. Cause the preparation of reclassification entries; and
c. Ensure the proper and complete information in the
supporting schedules of the Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs
accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
OIC-Municipal Accountant to prepare immediately the
reclassification entries to bring the affected accounts to their correct
balances.

We recommended and Municipal Mayor agreed to require the
OIC-Municipal Accountant to:

a. Review the Due to Other Funds account thoroughly and locate
and identify the errors; and
b. Adjust the account to its correct balance and recognize its
reciprocal account Due from Other Funds, if applicable.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the:

a. OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
• Review all book reconciling items and cause the
preparation of adjusting entries for those with available
documentation or reference;
• Summarize all reconciling items without available
supporting documents and references and submit the same
with a query to the COA Government Accountancy Sector
(GAS) as the appropriate body to address issues regarding
proper accounting treatment and what remedies to take in
order to clean the LGU’s books; and
• Prepare the entry to recognize the bank charges amounting
to ₱84,372.86 by debiting Government Equity and
crediting Cash In Bank, Local Currency, Current Account
under the Trust Fund; and

b. Municipal Treasurer to:
• Send notices to payees with stale checks for check
replacement; and
• Notify the Municipal Accountant of the details of the stale
checks and request for the preparation of the necessary
accounting entry to recognize the cancellation of stale
checks prior to the issuance of a replacement.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the:

a. Municipal Treasurer and the OIC, Municipal Accountant to
periodically reconcile their records to determine the accurate
Cash on Hand balances;
b. OIC-Municipal Accountant to cause the preparation of
adjusting entries upon identifying variances; and
c. Municipal Treasurer to:
• Deposit the collections with the authorized government
depository bank (AGDB) daily or not later than the next
banking day;
• Identify the exact amount that was lost due to theft and
correspondingly report to the OIC, Municipal Accountant,
for the preparation of the journal entry; and
• Adhere to the correct procedure for transferring funds
between bank accounts by issuing a check from the account
where the full real property tax was deposited and deposit
the same into the appropriate account.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Treasurer to establish comprehensive standby plans to
address any obstacles and ensure successful collection for the
adequate funding of all the Municipality’s programs and projects.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the
OIC-Municipal Accountant to:

a. Prioritize the retrieval of all necessary documents pertaining
to the unidentified CIP balance; and
b. Immediately initiate account reconciliation and prepare
supporting schedules for ongoing monitoring to enable
accurate adjustments in the books and ensure the relevant
accounts reflect the correct balances.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
OIC-Municipal Accountant to:

a. Determine the useful life of the Intangible Assets and compute
for the amortization expense from the time the asset was made
available for use; and
b. Prepare journal entries to bring the Intangible Assets to its
correct balance at a given period.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the
OIC-Municipal Accountant to:

a. Coordinate with the Municipal Budget Office, to ensure that
recorded obligations in the books of accounts are duly
supported with complete and necessary documents to support
the validity of the obligations;
b. Revert the payables totaling ₱2.511 million, which had been
outstanding for more than two years without actual claims, to
unappropriated surplus of the General Fund pursuant to
Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445;
c. Reclassify the Accounts Payable totaling ₱3.402 million from
Current to Non-Current Liability under Other Payables
account in consonance with Section 80 of IPSAS 1; and
d. Prepare an adjusting entry to correct the abnormal balance of
the Accounts Payable.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to require the
OIC-Municipal Accountant to:

a. Conduct a thorough review and analysis of Due to Officers and
Employees account and effect the necessary adjustments to
reclassify erroneously recognized Due to Officers and
Employees to Other Payables account;
b. Reclassify payables aged more than one year as Non-Current
Liability; and
c. Analyze and confirm outstanding payables aged two years or
more with no valid claims filed by identified creditors, for
reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the
OIC-Municipal Accountant to prioritize the GSIS reconciliation
process to mitigate the potential adverse impacts on
employee-members' availment of GSIS benefits.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:

a. Require the Human Resource Management Officer to
determine the cost or money value of the accumulated leave
credits of incumbent personnel and submit them to the OIC,
Municipal Accountant; and
b. Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
• Take up the gradual accrual of the earned leave benefits
upon determination of the cost or money value of the
accumulated leave credits, starting with the amounts to be
monetized for the year and the upcoming terminal leave
benefits of those employees planning to retire within that
year; and
• Disclose unaccrued earned leave benefits in the succeeding
years’ Notes to Financial Statements, providing all
necessary details and a recognition schedule.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:

a. Direct the HRMO to conduct Training Needs Assessment at
 the start of each year in order to determine essential
trainings/seminars to help improve the performance of LGU
personnel;
b. Direct all Department Heads to refrain from sending
personnel to training/seminars/conventions that are not in line
with their core functions;
c. Formulate an internal policy on the attendance of personnel to
trainings/seminars/conventions; and
d. Ensure that personnel sent to specialized trainings are
properly designated to perform the corresponding function
through the issuance of an office order.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:

a. Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
• Send letters or notices to the concerned suppliers or
contractors informing them of their retention money and
performance bond are due for refund, and ask them to
submit letter requests for the refund;
• Commence retrieving or gathering of documents of the
suppliers or contractors whose deliveries of
goods/rendition of services/completion of infrastructure
projects are beyond the warranty periods, with full or final
acceptance of deliveries and payments, to serve as
supporting documents for the refund; and
• Process the payments for the return of retention money
and performance bonds; and
b. Install monitoring mechanism on retention money and
performance bonds for the timely return of these securities to
suppliers.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:

a. Direct the Municipal Budget Officer and OIC-Municipal
Accountant to refrain from allocating funds and disbursing
funds for the repairs and maintenance of turned-over
Barangay Day Care Centers; and
b. Direct the Municipal Engineer to prepare the Memorandum
of Agreement for all turned-over Day Care Centers, explicitly
including a provision for the Municipality to cover repairs and
maintenance as assistance to the Barangays.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:

a. Direct the Municipal Engineer to:
• Strictly follow the schedules for development project
implementation to ensure the timely delivery of services
to the public; and
• Coordinate with Municipal Planning and Development
Officer in ensuring that savings realized from prior
years’ development projects are reprogrammed in the
succeeding year;
b. Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to:
• Gather and organize the available documents regarding
the Barangay Ungka II multi-purpose building and gym
for the immediate payment of the contractor once the
land title or deed of donation is available; and
• Coordinate with the Punong Barangay of Barangay
Ungka II for the submission of the land title or deed of
donation; and
c. Instruct the Municipal Planning Development Office in
coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer to monitor
the savings of the Development Funds and ensure that such
are reprogrammed to other development projects in the
succeeding year.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
GAD Focal Person to ensure the submission of the
DILG-endorsed GPB and GAD AR to the Audit Team within the
prescribed period.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
GAD Focal Person to ensure that the GPB and GAD AR
performance indicators and targets reflect the specific number of
male and female beneficiaries who will/are directly benefit/ed from
the implemented GAD Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs).

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to
refrain from reprogramming the savings from capital outlay
projects to MOOE in the succeeding year’s budget preparation and
ensure that proper coordination is made with the Municipal Budget
Officer and OIC, Municipal Accountant to properly determine the
expense class of the reprogrammed projects, programs and
activities before the approval of DRRM budget.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor and the LSB agreed to
ensure that the SEF plans and budget are effectively and efficiently
executed and optimally expended.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the
Municipal Engineer to:

a. Ensure the submission of a comprehensive list of planned and
ongoing Municipality’s GPPA at the start of each year to the
Audit Team; and
b. Prepare the infrastructure monitoring reports in the required
format/template containing the essential information about
the GPPAs implementation in reference to COA Circular No.
2013-004.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:

a. Direct the HRMO to:
• Formulate the hiring policies for the Job
Order/Contractual personnel, ensure the conduct of
manpower needs assessment and staffing patterns, and
prepare the standardized duties and responsibilities for
each designation; and
• Prepare the payroll certification for approval of the
Municipal Mayor that the services rendered are not
performed by plantillla personnel; and
b. Direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to require the
submission of the payroll certification before the processing of
the payments for Job Order/Contractual personnel.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the OIC-Municipal Accountant to: a. Conduct validation of the Unexpended balances as of December 31, 2022 and determine if there are concerned Source Agencies with valid claims. Return/remit to Source Agencies any unexpended amount of fund transfer after project completion pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994; and b. Reconcile with Source Agencies unused fund transfers, prepare adjusting entry for reconciling items and initiate the filing of write-off for undocumented dormant accounts taking into consideration the requirements as provided in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: The management coordinated with the appropriate agencies and was awaiting further instructions regarding the utilization of the funds. For PDAF, the unexpended balance was returned to the Bureau of the Treasury.
observations.reason: For PDAF, unexpended balance returned to the Bureau of the Treasury; coordination with source agencies ongoing for other items.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Council, Municipal Budget Officer and Department Heads to evaluate the necessity of the identified priority PPAs, which to date remained unimplemented. Otherwise, reprogram the amount together with the savings generated from completed PPAs in accordance with Section 3.2.2 of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 1, and to henceforth, properly plan and execute all procurement activities pertaining thereto to ensure the timely implementation and completion in accordance with Section 3.2.3 of the same circular, in order to ensure the judicious delivery of services to the constituents.
observations.managementAction: The management indicated that the identified priority PPAs were evaluated and reprogrammed where necessary and that procurement activities will be properly planned and executed to ensure timely implementation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the Municipal CBMS Coordinating Board to augment the manpower needs of the program to supplement the current team’s capabilities and to ensure the LGU’s compliance on the timely implementation thereof.
observations.managementAction: The management complied and augmented manpower to support the CBMS program and ensured timely implementation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct department heads to plan and identify PPAs that will address GAD issues relevant to their office mandate and staff, monitor the results thereof, and report on results. We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require the GAD Focal Point System to comply with JMC No. 2013-01 by informing the DILG Regional Office in writing should changes in the DILG-approved GPB occur and/or if there is a need to implement additional PPAs relevant to current gender issues or GAD-related undertakings.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed and instructed concerned department heads to plan and identify relevant PPAs and monitor their results; the GAD Focal Point System was required to inform the DILG Regional Office of changes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to ensure that future procurement trainings or capacity development programs attended by BAC, its Secretariat, TWG members, and employees from the procurement unit must either be organized by GPPB-TSO or with resource persons from the GPPB recognized trainers.
observations.managementAction: Management ensured that future procurement trainings attended by BAC, Secretariat, TWG and procurement employees will be organized by recognized GPPB trainers or GPPB-TSO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to: a. Formulate and adopt internal rules and procedures on the rendition of overtime services and payment of night shift differential of their respective personnel based on the policies and guidelines under CSC-DBM JC No. 02, s. 2015 and CSC-DBM-GCG JC No. 01, s. 2023 and submit the same to DBM and CSC; b. Comply with requirements provided by CSC-DBM JC No. 02, series of 2015 in determining the hourly rate; c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that disbursements of overtime and night shift differentials are covered with proper and complete documentation and ascertain that daily time records properly reflect the time of arrival and departure particularly during night shift; and d. Direct Department Heads to carefully analyze the monthly schedule of employees to avoid understaffing or overloading of public health workers. Changes in schedule or shift are to be approved by the immediate supervisor and head of the agency.
observations.managementAction: Management prepared internal rules/procedures and submitted COE No. 2022-015RD dated October 19, 2022. Departments were directed to ensure compliance and proper documentation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the BAC to provide a written justification as to the irregularities in the conduct of post-qualification, such that the bidder to whom the contract was awarded was not able to attach a signed sales invoice, whereas the bidder with the lowest calculated bid was disqualified for failure to include the same document.
observations.managementAction: Management ensured that the irregularity was addressed and provided the required justification; BAC actions were adjusted accordingly.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require concerned officials and employees to: a. Submit a certified list approved and noted by the Municipal Health Officer of the individuals who spent New Year’s Eve at the quarantine facilities, for verification; and b. Submit duly noted office orders of LGU employees and personnel assigned at the quarantine facilities and checkpoints.
observations.managementAction: Management implemented the COE No. 2022-015RD dated October 19, 2022 and submitted required documents; disallowed items, if any, were refunded by accountable officials and employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and Management agreed to prepare a liquidation report, complete with official receipts for the rice purchased and financial assistance given to barangays for cash donations received, and submit to this Office.
observations.managementAction: Management prepared and submitted liquidation reports and supporting official receipts to COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to require both suppliers for the said procurement to submit their Licenses to Operate as a Medical Device Distributor/Wholesaler from the FDA and Certificates of Registration from the BIR to prove their legal and technical capacity to enter into contract with the Municipality for the said project and to ensure that the rapid test kits purchased were FDA approved.
observations.managementAction: Management required suppliers to submit licenses and certificates to ensure procurement from legally and technically capable suppliers; corrective measures were implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and Management agreed that the COE No. 2022-015RD dated October 19, 2022 be issued and that the excess amount of professional fees disbursed as irregular expenditures be duly refunded by accountable officials and employees.
observations.managementAction: COE No. 2022-015RD dated October 19, 2022 was issued; accountable officials and employees refunded the excess amounts as required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to refund the excess amount of Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2013 totaling ₱3.560 million paid to the municipal officials and employees.
observations.managementAction: ND No. 2015-001-100(2013) was issued and the excess amount was refunded.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that all Municipal Officials and Employees refund the PEI amounting to ₱4.446 million in excess of its PS limitation since it is contrary to the provisions of Section 325 (a) of RA 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.
observations.managementAction: ND No. 2016-001-100(2014) was issued and the excess amount was refunded by officials and employees.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to direct the:

Inventory Committee to properly plan and conduct the physical count of all PPEs and ensure timely and complete submission of RPCPPE to the Office of the Auditor; and

b.	OIC, General Services Officer and the OIC, Municipal Accountant to work together in reconciling and updating both their records. Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments on any discrepancies noted.

Likewise, we recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to finally establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to secure the approval of the BLGF so that the LGU can continue to maintain its existing current account with the PNB; and

Instruct the OIC, Municipal Accountant to summarize all reconciling items without available supporting documents and references, and submit the same with a query to the COA Government Accountancy Sector (GAS) for its appropriate accounting treatment and remedies to clean the LGU’s books.

Require both suppliers for the submission of their Licenses to Operate as a Medical Devise Distributor/Wholesaler from the FDA and Certificates of Registration from the BIR to prove their legal and technical capacity to enter into contract with the Municipality for the said project and to ensure that the rapid test kits purchased were FDA approved;

Provide justification as to the efficiency of negotiating from the supplier instead of directly negotiating from an FDA authorized one as stipulated in the JVA; and

Provide justification as to the absence of the required recommendation to award the contract to the supplier and the corresponding NOA.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant:

To see to it that requests for advance payment by contractors are properly supported with the financial instruments required under Section 4, Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 before payments are made; and

To ensure that retention money is withheld before payments are made to contractors and/or make certain that the financial instruments submitted to substitute the retention money are in the form and amount prescribed under Section 6, Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and must meet the conditions stated therein before substitution is allowed.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the MDRRMC to prepare and submit the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) indicating the detailed PPAs to be funded, and include the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years under a separate caption for ease of monitoring of project implementation and evaluation.

We recommended and Management agreed to adhere to the provisions prescribed in the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. No. 9184 pertaining to warranty security, not only for infrastructure projects but also for the procurement of goods. Henceforth, ensure that future procurements are deducted with the 1% retention money or require a special bank guarantee of the same amount, otherwise, any expenses due to defects noted within the warranty period should be charged to concerned approving officials.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive must seek first a written conformity and acquiescence of the Solicitor General and the written concurrence of the Commission on Audit before hiring a private lawyer and strictly comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 98-002.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive seek the services of the Provincial Legal Officer or Provincial Prosecutor in the absence of the Municipal Legal Officer to attend to its legal affairs, including representation in court.

We further recommended that the amount disbursed as professional fees of the legal advisor, which are considered to be illegal and irregular expenditures be duly refunded by accountable officials and employees.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that retention money is withheld in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR before payment is made to the contractors.

We recommended that the LDRRMO prepare its LDRRMFIP reflecting the amounts allocated for the PPAs charged from the unexpended prior years’ LDRRMF in the trust fund books and the additional/ reprogrammed PPAs for implementation as approved by LCE and Local Sanggunian, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1, and for the proper implementation and monitoring of DRRM funds.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to enhance collection efforts so that RPT and SET Receivables are realized, and tax delinquencies are collected; and

Direct the Municipal Assessor to conduct actual inspection and inventory of real properties in the municipality so that a proper classification, appraisal and assessment is made, and a more attainable target of RPT and SET Receivables is set.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to strictly enforce collection of unpaid market stall rentals and initiate strategies to collect the same on a timely basis so that the revenue generating potential of market operations is optimized.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive convene with the Local School Board and prepare the three-year program for the strategic prioritization of policies in the allocation and use of the Special Education Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the employees and officials concerned to submit copies of the perfected contracts and supporting documents within the prescribed period in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009, for the timely conduct of contract review and other necessary audit procedures.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and concerned officials:

Prepare an Organizational Structure and Staffing Pattern Plan, and formulate policies and guidelines so that the necessity and propriety of hiring job order employees are justified.

Incorporate in the contract the services to be rendered and the number of man hours, and quantity and duration of the works to be performed by the job order personnel and require them to submit quantified accomplishment reports.

Immediately strengthen the controls in the hiring of JOs by requiring them to submit personal records, undergo interview, and document performance evaluation to give assurance that the risk identified above is addressed.

Discontinue hiring individuals prohibited under Section 4 of CSC Resolution No. 020790, and ensure that compensation of JOs are paid on a daily basis rather than monthly.

In coordination with the department heads, assess and review the necessity of hiring job orders by considering the existence of regular employees who could perform some of the contracted work. Maximize the services of the regular employees and minimize the hiring of job orders.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials and employees to take immediate action and comply with the schedule of GAD planning and budgeting by submitting the GPB to the PPDO for review within the period prescribed in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2016-01.

We recommended therefore, that the Local Chief Executive direct the: 

OIC-Municipal Accountant to review all outstanding and dormant trust liabilities, and perform a reconciliation with the Source Agencies for the balances. 


b. Acting Municipal Treasurer to immediately cause the return of funds should the Source Agencies demand for it; otherwise, seek written approval so that the balances will be transferred to the general fund to be utilized for another purpose.

We recommended therefore, that the Local Chief Executive direct the Local Disaster Risk Management Officer to prepare and submit the annual LDRRMFIP according to the guidelines embodied in COA 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

Moreover, we recommended that the LDRRMFIP be prepared as to include the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF of the previous years so that funds will be appropriately monitored and ensure its proper use.

We recommended that the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board review and update its 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan in accordance with the provision of RA 9003 to ensure the viability, sustainability and effectiveness of all programs, projects and activities included therein.

We recommended that the Solid Waste Management Board prepare its own monitoring of the status of implementation and utilization of funds appropriated for solid waste management programs/ projects/ activities as embodied in the annual budget.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

a.	Direct the Municipal Accountant to exert more effort in evaluating and identifying the causes of the variance between these reciprocal accounts and effect the necessary correcting entries immediately.

We recommended that the Municipality’s LCPC prepare the Annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children and the Local Investment Plan for Children to serve as guide for an effective implementation of its programs/ projects/ activities. It should also be included in the Annual Investment Program of the Municipality to ensure its funding.
We recommended the refund of the excess amount of Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2013 totaling ₱3,559,500.00 paid to the municipal officials and employees.

We recommended that all Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the PEI amounting to ₱4,446,000.00 in excess of its PS limitation granted to its employees since it is contrary to the provisions of Section 325 (a) of RA 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the BAC and the Municipal Engineer to properly plan and schedule all procurement activities pertaining to development projects for their timely implementation and completion.

We also recommended that the Municipal Development Council and the Municipal Engineer regularly monitor the status of project implementation. The MDC should also ensure to maximize the use of savings from completed PPAs and to evaluate the necessity of the unimplemented PPAs so that it can be utilized to fund other identified priority development PPAs in accordance with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We further recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to require the submission of official receipt from the contractor of the project Construction of Drainage System at Poblacion Area, in accordance with BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 2-2014, to avoid suspension of transactions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Require the MENRO to pre-number the Daily Garbage Collection Slip and to control/monitor the issuance thereof to garbage collectors. Pre-numbered slips in three copies should be issued in strict numerical sequence and all copies should be exact copies or carbon reproduction in all respects of the original.

Instruct the MENRO to furnish the Municipal Accountant a daily abstract of billings duly supported with a copy of the issued Daily Garbage Collection Slips for the setting up of Accounts Receivable.

Oblige the MENRO to perform a daily reconciliation of the quantity of slips issued to garbage collectors vis-à-vis the quantity issued to clients and the balance on hand or the unused slips to ensure completeness of the recorded billings.

Require the monthly reconciliation of recorded collection between the MENRO and the Municipal Accountant.

Instruct the Municipal Accountant and the MENRO to monitor the receivables from garbage fees and enforce the collection thereof.

We therefore recommended for the Municipality to ensure that the specifications and other terms necessary to meet their needs and its end-user shall be reflected as provided for in Section 17.2 of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of the R.A. No. 9184. 

Moreover, we recommended for the MDRRMO to be prudent in monitoring, inventory and distribution of stockpiled goods to avoid wastage of the items.

Likewise, we recommended for the MDRRMO to re-evaluate the necessity of maintaining food packages and the timing of procuring them in preparation of the possibility that a calamity will occur, so that unnecessary storage of food items will be avoided and the resources will only be utilized to its intended purpose.

We recommended that the OIC- Municipal Accountant draw the appropriate journal entry/entries to recognize the correct inventory balance for welfare goods at year-end and recognize accordingly the amount expensed for goods already distributed and lost, pursuant to the guidelines set forth in IPSAS 12, Volume I.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant draw the appropriate journal entry/entries to record the purchase of equipment to the appropriate Property Plant Equipment account pursuant to the guidelines set forth in IPSAS 17, Volume II so that assets balance will be properly presented in the books of the agency.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant compute the liquidated damages pertaining to the number of days complete delivery of items was delayed in accordance with Section 68 and Annex D of the 2016 IRR of RA No. 9184. 

Likewise, we recommended that the Municipality perform the necessary procedures to collect the amount due from the supplier pertaining to liquidated damages according to the foregoing provisions.

We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant draw up the necessary journal entry/entries to reflect the correct inventory balance in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to strictly enforce collection of unpaid market stall rentals and initiate strategies to collect the same on a timely basis so that the revenue generating potential of market operations is optimized.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Local School Board immediately stop the utilization of SEF for purposes other than those provided in RA 5447 and RA 7160 and in harmony with DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to cease from designating casual and job order personnel as collecting officers since no employer-employee relationship exists between them and the LGU, and ensure that all accountable officers assigned to collect revenues and receipts are properly bonded in accordance with Section 101 of P.D. No. 1445, so that government funds are properly safeguarded.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the: 

OIC-Municipal Accountant to review all outstanding and dormant trust liabilities, and perform a reconciliation with the Source Agencies for the balances. 

b. Acting Municipal Treasurer to immediately cause the return of funds should the Source Agencies demand for it; otherwise, seek written approval so that the balances will be transferred to the general fund to be utilized for another purpose.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) that the required documents for the proposed location of the projects are first complied with before awarding of contract in accordance with Section 17.6 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to avoid delays in the completion of projects and risk of contract termination by the contractor.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive coordinate with the recipient barangays to hasten the possession of the required documents.

We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer ensure that a detailed site investigation/ inspection is conducted prior to the implementation of the projects so as to avoid suspension of construction works for a significant amount of time and to enable the timely delivery of convenience and benefits to the intended users.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Local Disaster Risk Management Officer and Municipal Budget Officer to properly charge the disbursements made against LDRRMF in accordance with DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and other related guidelines on the utilization of the LDRRM Fund, and prioritize the projects and programs which address disaster prevention, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.

Collections of monthly stall rentals and other market fees made by the PEMPC on October 16, 2017 up to the present should be verified by the Acting Municipal Treasurer so that the same may be demanded to be remitted to the LGU.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned continue to exert efforts to collect outstanding cash advances and unsettled receivables. Moreover, guidelines and procedures on the request for write-off of unliquidated cash advances must be adhered to should management decide to file a request.

We recommended that the Municipality’s LCPC prepare the Annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children and the Local Investment Plan for Children to serve as guide for an effective implementation of its programs/ projects/ activities. It should also be included in the Annual Investment Program of the municipality to ensure its funding.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive and the LDRRMO formulate feasible plans for PPAs to be included in the Municipality’s annual LDRRMPs and LDRRMIPs to maximize the utilization of the allocation for LDRRM Program, Projects and Activities. 

We likewise recommended that only priority projects be included in the DRRM programs that are congruent to LDRRM guidelines and objectives. 

We also recommended that the remaining unutilized LDRRMF that have lapsed the 5 year period be reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund and shall be made available for other socials services.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned persons to liquidate or refund immediately the cash advance granted for travel and other expenditures to avoid understating the expense and overstating the asset and equity accounts as well as, prevent the exposure of funds to risk of misapplication. Effort should be exerted to recover the cash advance granted or cause its liquidation/settlement by sending demand letters to the concerned persons.
We recommended the refund of the excess amount of Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2013 totaling ₱3,559,500.00 paid to the municipal officials and employees.

We recommended that all Municipal Officials and Employees cause the refund of the PEI amounting to ₱4,446,000.00 in excess of its PS limitation granted to its employees since it is contrary to the provisions of Section 325 (a) of RA 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.

We also recommended that the LGU management initiate and optimize the generation of resources and revenues, and ensure that all taxes and other revenues of the LGU are collected in order to increase the allowable PS level. Pertinent thereto, the Sangguniang Bayan should enact the corresponding ordinances levying taxes, fees and charges and prescribe the rates thereof and should come up with judicious and holistic evaluation of the reasonableness of projected revenues and other receipts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned ensure to:

a.	Establish the existence, condition, validity and accuracy of its fixed assets by conducting a physical inventory at least once a year in compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Section 156 of COA Circular No. 92-386 which prescribes the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the LGUs;

b.	Expedite the inventory-taking in accordance with the following prescribed procedures:

	Creation of Inventory Committee
	
	A committee shall be formed to take charge of the physical inventory-taking, consisting of the representative of the local chief executive as chairman, the general services officer, and the supply accountable officer of the department or office concerned.

	Property Identification and Coding

	Government Property shall be properly labeled and identified by the Inventory Committee. Inventory labels of special materials shall contain among others the individual property number to be provided by the LGU concerned. These numbers shall be uniformly printed in India ink, to achieve permanence and best results, and shall be posted on prominent but secure portion of the property for easy identification.

	Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE)

	An officer or employee who receives and is in possession or physical control of the property shall be issued an ARE. Such ARE shall be renewed every three (3) years or every time there is a change in accountability as required by NGAS, Volume II.

	Inventory Reports

	The report on the physical count of property, plant and equipment shall be prepared on the format prescribed by the NGAS, Volume II. The report shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year for the property, plant and equipment.

c.	Reconcile the inventory reports with the accounting and property records.

We further recommended that  the  Disposal  Committee  follow  the  disposal
procedures and accomplish the forms  to  be  used  in  reporting  disposable
supplies and property.

Recommendations

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive create an appraisal  committee
to come up with the appraised  value  of  the  donated  land  as  basis  for
recording thereof.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the  OIC-Municipal
Accountant to prepare journal entries to  recognize  the  value  of  donated
land once appraisal and valuation is done to ensure reliability of the  Land
account as presented in the financial statement.

Recommendation

We recommended  that the  Local  Chief  Executive  instruct  the  Municipal
Treasurer to strictly adhere to Section 4 of PD  No.  1445  and  established
rules, regulations and policies in financial transactions of LGUs to  ensure
that assets are safeguarded and loss of resources is prevented.

Recommendations

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct:


a. The OIC-Municipal  Accountant  to  cause  the  preparation  of  adjusting
journal  entries  of  reconciling  items  with  available  documentation  or
reference.  The  LGU  should  likewise  make  representations  to  PNB   for
correction and reversal of the debit memos and bank  charges  noted  in  the
BRS.

b. The Municipal Treasurer to maintain cashbooks accordingly  so  that  cash
balances are closely monitored to ensure  accuracy.  Reconciliation  of  the
cashbook balance with accounting records should  also  be  undertaken  every
end of the month in order for necessary corrections and  adjustments  to  be
taken up in the books in a timely manner.

Recommendation

We  recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the   concerned
officials and employees to secure approval  from  the  BLGF  to  maintain  a
depository  account  at  PNB  otherwise,  instruct  the   Acting   Municipal
Treasurer  to  transfer  the  said  account  to  an  authorized   government
depository bank or GFIs.

Recommendations

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct:


a. The Municipal Accountant to see to it that retention  money  is  withheld
   pursuant to Section 6, Annex E of RA No. 9184 before payments are made to
   contractors.


b. The Municipal Engineer to properly  verify  the  status  of  the  project
   implementation  before  requests  of  contractors  for  substitution   of
   retention money is allowed.


c. The Municipal Accountant to ensure that financial  instruments  submitted
   to substitute the retention money are in the form and amount  as  allowed
   under Section 6, Annex E of RA No. 9184.


d. The Municipal Engineer to see to it that a warranty  security  should  be
   issued  effective  for  one  year  from  the  date  of  issuance  of  the
   Certificate of Final Acceptance for the projects listed above and  should
   be returned only after the lapse of the said one year period. The posting
   of warranty security should be in the  form  and  amount  provided  under
   Section 62.2.3.3 of RA No. 9184 to guarantee  the  contractor’s  faithful
   performance of their responsibilities prescribed in  Section  62.2.3.1(a)
   of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

Recommendations

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE)  instruct  the  BAC  and
the Municipal  Engineer  to  properly  plan  and  schedule  all  procurement
activities   pertaining   to   development   projects   for   their   timely
implementation and completion.

We  also  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Development  Council  and   the
Municipal Engineer regularly monitor the status of  project  implementation.
The MDC should also ensure to maximize the use  of  savings  from  completed
PPAs and to evaluate the necessity of the unimplemented PPAs so that it  can
be  utilized  to  fund  other  identified  priority  development   PPAs   in
accordance with DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

We further recommended that the  LCE  direct  the  Municipal  Treasurer  and
Municipal Accountant to require the submission of official receipt from  the
contractor of the project  Construction  of  Drainage  System  at  Poblacion
Area, in accordance with BIR Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 2-2014,  to
avoid suspension of transactions.

Recommendations

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

a. Require the MENRO to pre-number the Daily Garbage Collection Slip and  to
   control/monitor the issuance thereof to garbage collectors.  Pre-numbered
   slips in three copies should be issued in strict numerical  sequence  and
   all copies should be exact copies or carbon reproduction in all  respects
   of the original.

b. Instruct the MENRO to furnish the Municipal Accountant a  daily  abstract
   of billings duly supported with  a  copy  of  the  issued  Daily  Garbage
   Collection Slips for the setting up of Accounts Receivable.

c. Oblige the MENRO to perform a daily reconciliation  of  the  quantity  of
   slips issued to garbage  collectors  vis-à-vis  the  quantity  issued  to
   clients  and  the  balance  on  hand  or  the  unused  slips  to   ensure
   completeness of the recorded billings.

d. Require the monthly reconciliation of  recorded  collection  between  the
   MENRO and the Municipal Accountant.

e.  Instruct  the  Municipal  Accountant  and  the  MENRO  to  monitor   the
   receivables from garbage fees and enforce the collection thereof.

Recommendations

We  therefore  recommended  for  the  Municipality  to   ensure   that   the
specifications and other terms necessary to meet their needs  and  its  end-
user shall be reflected as provided for in Section 17.2 of the 2016  Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations of the R.A. No. 9184.

Moreover, we recommended  for  the  MDRRMO  to  be  prudent  in  monitoring,
inventory and distribution of stockpiled  goods  to  avoid  wastage  of  the
items.

Likewise, we recommended for the MDRRMO  to  re-evaluate  the  necessity  of
maintaining food packages and the timing of procuring  them  in  preparation
of the possibility that a calamity will occur, so that  unnecessary  storage
of food items will be avoided and the resources will  only  be  utilized  to
its intended purpose.

Recommendation

We recommended that the  OIC-  Municipal  Accountant  draw  the  appropriate
journal  entry/entries  to  recognize  the  correct  inventory  balance  for
welfare goods at year-end and recognize accordingly the amount expensed  for
goods already distributed and lost, pursuant to the guidelines set forth  in
IPSAS 12, Volume I.

Recommendation

We recommended that the LDRRMO prepare its LDRRMFIP reflecting the  amounts
allocated for the PPAs charged from the unexpended prior years’  LDRRMF  in
the  trust  fund   books   and   the   additional/reprogrammed   PPAs   for
implementation as approved by LCE and Local Sanggunian, in compliance  with
COA Circular No. 2012-002 and NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1, and  for
the proper implementation and monitoring of DRRM funds.

Recommendation

We recommended  that  the  OIC-Municipal  Accountant  draw  the  appropriate
journal  entry/entries  to  record  the  purchase  of   equipment   to   the
appropriate Property Plant Equipment account pursuant to the guidelines  set
forth in IPSAS 17, Volume  II  so  that  assets  balance  will  be  properly
presented in the books of the agency.

Recommendations

We recommended that the  OIC-Municipal  Accountant  compute  the  liquidated
damages pertaining to the number of days  complete  delivery  of  items  was
delayed in accordance with Section 68 and Annex D of the 2016 IRR of RA  No.
9184.


Likewise,  we  recommended  that  the  Municipality  perform  the  necessary
procedures to collect  the  amount  due  from  the  supplier  pertaining  to
liquidated damages according to the foregoing provisions.

We also recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant draw up the  necessary
journal entry/entries to  reflect  the  correct  inventory  balance  in the
financial statements.

Recommendations

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:


a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer to enhance collection efforts so that  RPT
   and SET Receivables are realized, and tax  delinquencies  are  collected,
   and to avail of the remedies by administrative action to enforce  payment
   on delinquent Real Property Taxes  thru  levy  on  real  property  or  by
   judicial action as provided in Section 256 of RA 7160; and


b. Direct the Municipal Assessor to conduct actual inspection and  inventory
   of real properties in the Municipality so that a  proper  classification,
   appraisal and assessment is made, and a more attainable target of RPT and
   SET Receivables is set.

Recommendation

We  recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the   Municipal
Treasurer to strictly enforce collection of unpaid market stall rentals  and
initiate strategies to collect the same  on  a  timely  basis  so  that  the
revenue generating potential of market operations is optimized.

Recommendation

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and  the  Local  School  Board
immediately stop the utilization  of SEF  for  purposes  other  than  those
provided in RA 5447 and RA 7160 and in  harmony  with  DepEd-DBM-DILG  Joint
Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

Recommendation

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive convene with the Local  School
Board and prepare the three-year program for  the  strategic  prioritization
of policies in the allocation and use of the Special Education Fund.

Recommendation

We recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  instruct  the  Municipal
Treasurer to cease from  designating  casual  and  job  order  personnel  as
collecting officers since no employer-employee relationship  exists  between
them and the LGU, and ensure  that  all  accountable  officers  assigned  to
collect revenues  and  receipts  are  properly  bonded  in  accordance  with
Section 101 of  P.D.  No.  1445,  so  that  government  funds  are  properly
safeguarded.

Recommendation

We recommended that the Local Chief  Executive  require  the  employees  and
officials  concerned  to  submit  copies  of  the  perfected  contracts  and
supporting documents within the prescribed period  in  compliance  with  COA
Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009, for  the  timely  conduct  of
contract review and other necessary audit procedures.

Recommendations

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and concerned officials:

a. Prepare an  Organizational  Structure  and  Staffing  Pattern  Plan,  and
   formulate policies and guidelines so that the necessity and propriety  of
   hiring job order employees are justified.

b. Incorporate in the contract the services to be rendered  and  the  number
   of man hours, and quantity and duration of the works to be  performed  by
   the  job  order  personnel  and  require  them   to   submit   quantified
   accomplishment reports.

c. Immediately strengthen the controls in the hiring  of  JOs  by  requiring
   them  to  submit  personal  records,  undergo  interview,  and   document
   performance evaluation to give assurance that the risk  identified above
   is addressed.


d.  Discontinue  hiring  individuals  prohibited  under  Section  4  of  CSC
   Resolution No. 020790, and ensure that compensation of JOs are paid on  a
   daily basis rather than monthly.


e. In  coordination  with  the  department  heads,  assess  and  review  the
   necessity of hiring job orders by considering the  existence  of  regular
   employees who could perform some of the  contracted  work.  Maximize  the
   services of the regular employees and minimize the hiring of job orders.

f. Properly classify the employment status of the appointed  traffic  aides,
   daycare workers and cemetery caretaker in accordance with  civil  service
   rules and regulations.

We recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  instruct  the  concerned
officials and employees to comply with the  schedule  of  GAD  planning  and
budgeting by submitting the GPB to the PPDO for  review  within  the  period
prescribed in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2016-01.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned ensure that at the start of every budget period, the end-user units of the LGU shall prepare their respective PPMP in the prescribed format for their different programs, activities and projects (PAPs) in accordance with Sections 7.1 to 7.4 of The 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) post the Notices of Award and to Proceed, including the approved contract in the PhilGEPS website in accordance with Section 37 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to ensure transparency of activities in all stages of procurement process. A printout should also be attached in the disbursement vouchers as proof of posting.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and all others concerned ensure that the disbursements for advance payments to contractors are duly accompanied by a guarantee or bond in the form and amount specified under Section 4, Annex E, of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to serve as security and to mitigate/avoid possible loss on the part of the municipality in case the contractor fails to meet its contractual obligations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to require the submission of a Statement of Work Accomplishment as of target completion date for final billings of delayed projects and review the computations of liquidated damages to ensure its propriety before payment is made.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer, during monitoring of the status of implementation of projects, see to it that delays incurred beyond the threshold of ten percent (10%) of the specified contract time plus any time extension duly granted be properly evaluated so that appropriate course of action will be made to the concerned contractor, in accordance with Section 8.4 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee to ensure that contractors and suppliers comply with the documentary requirements prior to award of contracts.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Accountant not to process payments to contractors and suppliers in the absence of the required supporting documents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials and employees to be meticulous and judicious in the preparation of their Annual Procurement Plan so that procurements made by the municipality are properly planned and executed, and procurements made through alternative methods do not result to splitting of government contracts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials and employees to honor existing contractual agreements, and refrain from acquiring the services of suppliers who are not covered with legal and binding agreements, to avoid breach of contract and possible legal ramifications as well as to prevent the incurrence of irregular expenditures.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive be more judicious and frugal in its expenses for meals and snacks, and come up with a policy that would mandate the application of economy measures in order to prevent uneconomical spending.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned officials and employees to strictly adhere to the provisions of the IRR of RA 9433 by identifying and determining specific positions that are eligible to receive hazard pay with descriptions as to actual exposure to occupational risks including the duration of such exposure and assignment to places mentioned in Section 16.1.1 of the IRR.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct concerned officials and employees to:

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to ensure that approved SB Resolutions are secured prior to processing of payments for projects appropriated under programs/projects/activities stated in generic terms in the annual budget and Annual Procurement Plan.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Development Council and concerned offices to be more specific in the identification of priority projects and programs to be charged against the 20% MDF such that the projects included in the APP and the annual budget already contains sufficient details and costing.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned ensure that all projects sourced out of the 20% MDF are included in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) in the prescribed format, in accordance with Sections 7.1 and 7.2 of RA No. 9184.

We also recommended that proper planning is to be employed in the identification of projects to be included in the annual budget of the municipality and ensure the timely implementation thereof in accordance with the time frames set in the APP to allow the constituents the immediate enjoyment of the benefits that could be derived therefrom.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Local Disaster Risk Management Officer and Municipal Budget Officer to properly charge the disbursements made against LDRRMF in accordance with DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and other related guidelines on the utilization of the LDRRM Fund, and prioritize the projects and programs which address disaster prevention, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.

We recommended that the Municipality reorganize its Solid Waste Management Board to include the Chairperson of the Sangguniang Kabataan Federation and a representative of the manufacturing or packaging industry to conform with the membership requirement under RA 9003 and to optimize the involvement of youth and other industry sectors in solid waste management activities of the municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive thru the Sangguniang Bayan issue and enforce ordinances related to the establishment of Materials Recovery Facility in every barangay or cluster of barangays in compliance with Section 32 of RA 9003 to effect the proper segregation, collection and disposal of solid wastes.

We recommended that the Solid Waste Management Board prepare its own monitoring of the status of implementation and utilization of funds appropriated for solid waste management programs/projects/activities as embodied in the annual budget.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to implement proper identification of the AIP Code in the recording of obligations against appropriations per programs/projects/activities to enable proper monitoring of budget utilization and to effectively evaluate the extent of implementation of various programs and projects of the municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned ensure to:

a.	Establish the existence, condition, validity and accuracy of its fixed assets by conducting a physical inventory at least once a year in compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Section 156 of COA Circular No. 92-386 which prescribes the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the LGUs;

b.	Expedite the inventory-taking in accordance with the following prescribed procedures:

	Creation of Inventory Committee
	
A committee shall be formed to take charge of the physical inventory-taking, consisting of the representative of the local chief executive as chairman, the general services officer, and the supply accountable officer of the department or office concerned.

	Property Identification and Coding

	Government Property shall be properly labeled and identified by the Inventory Committee. Inventory labels of special materials shall contain among others the individual property number to be provided by the LGU concerned. These numbers shall be uniformly printed in India ink, to achieve permanence and best results, and shall be posted on prominent but secure portion of the property for easy identification.

	Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE)

	An officer or employee who receives and is in possession or physical control of the property shall be issued an ARE. Such ARE shall be renewed every three (3) years or every time there is a change in accountability as required by NGAS, Volume II.

	Inventory Reports

	The report on the physical count of property, plant and equipment shall be prepared on the format prescribed by the NGAS, Volume II. The report shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year for the property, plant and equipment.
