We recommended that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  and  officials  concerned ensure that at the start of every budget period, the end-user units  of  the LGU shall prepare their respective PPMP in the prescribed format  for their different programs,  activities  and  projects  (PAPs)  in  accordance  with Sections 7.1 to 7.4 of The 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) post the Notices  of Award and to Proceed,  including  the  approved  contract  in  the  PhilGEPS website in accordance with Section 37 of the Revised IRR of RA No.  9184  to ensure transparency of activities in all stages of  procurement  process.  A printout should also be attached in the disbursement vouchers  as  proof  of posting.

We recommended that  the  Municipal  Accountant  and  all  others  concerned ensure that the disbursements for advance payments to contractors  are  duly accompanied by a guarantee or bond in the form and  amount  specified  under  Section 4, Annex E, of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to serve  as  security  and  to mitigate/avoid possible loss on the part of the municipality in  case the contractor fails to meet its contractual obligations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Bids  and  Awards Committee (BAC) that the required documents for  the  proposed  location  of  the projects  are  first  complied  with  before  awarding  of  contract in accordance with Section 17.6 of the Revised IRR of  RA  No.  9184  to  avoid delays in the completion of projects and risk  of  contract  termination  by the contractor.

We also recommended that the  Local  Chief  Executive  coordinate  with  the recipient barangays to hasten the possession of the required documents.

We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer ensure  that  a detailed site investigation/inspection is conducted prior to  the  implementation  of the projects  so  as  to  avoid  suspension  of  construction  works  for  a significant amount of time and to enable the timely delivery of  convenience and benefits to the intended users.

We recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  instruct  the  Municipal Accountant to require the submission of a Statement of  Work  Accomplishment as of target completion date for final  billings  of  delayed  projects  and review the computations  of  liquidated  damages  to  ensure  its  propriety before payment is made.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer, during  monitoring  of  the status of implementation of projects, see to it that delays incurred  beyond the threshold of ten percent (10%) of the specified contract time  plus  any time extension duly  granted  be  properly  evaluated  so  that  appropriate course of action will be made to the  concerned  contractor,  in  accordance with Section 8.4 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We  recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the   Municipal Accountant to see to it that retention money is withheld in accordance  with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR before payment is made  to the contractors.

We also recommended  that  the  status  of  the  project  implementation  is properly verified by the Municipal Engineer before requests  of  contractors for the substitution  of  the  retention  money  is  allowed  and  that  the Municipal Accountant should make sure that it is supported with a report  or certification  that  the  project  is  implemented  on   schedule   and is satisfactorily undertaken.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Accountant see  to  it  that  all the required securities submitted by the  contractors  are  properly  issued and are valid for the required duration and should  serve  the  purpose  for which it was issued, in accordance with pertinent provisions of the  Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant see  to  it  that  upon issuance of the Certificate of Final  Acceptance  for  the  projects  listed above, it should require the contractors the posting  of  warranty  security in the form and amount  provided  under  Section  62.2.3.3  of  RA  9184  to guarantee faithful  performance  of  their  responsibilities  prescribed  in Section 62.2.3.1(a) of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct  the  Bids  and  Awards Committee  to  ensure  that  contractors  and  suppliers  comply  with   the documentary requirements prior to award of contracts.

We also recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  instruct  the  OIC-Municipal Accountant not to process payments to  contractors  and  suppliers in the absence of the required supporting documents.

We  recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the   concerned officials and employees to be meticulous and judicious  in  the  preparation of  their  Annual  Procurement  Plan  so  that  procurements  made  by   the municipality are  properly  planned  and  executed,  and  procurements  made through alternative  methods  do  not  result  to  splitting  of  government contracts.

We  recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the   concerned officials and  employees  to  honor  existing  contractual  agreements,  and refrain from acquiring the services of suppliers who are  not  covered  with legal and binding agreements, to  avoid  breach  of  contract  and  possible legal ramifications as well  as  to  prevent  the  incurrence  of  irregular expenditures.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive  be  more  judicious  and frugal in its expenses for meals and snacks, and come up with a policy  that would mandate the application  of  economy  measures  in  order  to  prevent uneconomical spending.

We  recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the   concerned officials and employees to strictly adhere to the provisions of the  IRR  of RA 9433 by identifying and determining specific positions that are  eligible to  receive  hazard  pay  with  descriptions  as  to  actual   exposure   to occupational risks including the duration of such  exposure  and  assignment to places mentioned in Section 16.1.1 of the IRR.

We also recommended that the Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the  concerned officials and employees to discontinue  the  grant  of  hazard  pay  to  the MDRRMO due to his ineligibility to receive the said benefit.

We recommended that the Local Chief  Executive  direct  concerned  officials and employees to:


     a. Expedite the process of securing deeds of usufruct  for  the  roads which are the subject of  the  program  so  that  ownership  and/or authority to use such are established.


     b. Install a Community Billboard within the premises of the  municipal hall, and at the project location showing the status of the project in compliance with Sections 6.5.4.1 and 6.5.4.2 of DILG MC No. 2018-61 dated May 3, 2018.


     c. Henceforth, comply with all policy guidelines and procedures in the implementation  of  programs  funded  through  national  government assistance to avoid disqualification on future projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct  the  Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to ensure that approved SB  Resolutions are secured prior to processing of payments for projects appropriated  under programs/projects/activities stated in generic terms in  the  annual  budget and Annual Procurement Plan.


We also recommended that the Local Chief  Executive  require  the  Municipal Development Council and  concerned  offices  to  be  more  specific  in  the identification of priority projects and programs to be charged  against  the 20% MDF such that the projects included in the APP  and  the  annual  budget already contains sufficient details and costing.

We recommended that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  and  officials  concerned ensure that all projects sourced out of the 20%  MDF  are  included  in  the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) in the prescribed format, in  accordance  with Sections 7.1 and 7.2 of RA No. 9184.

We  also  recommended  that  proper  planning  is  to  be  employed  in  the identification of projects to be  included  in  the  annual  budget  of  the municipality and ensure the  timely  implementation  thereof  in  accordance with the time frames set in the APP to allow the constituents the  immediate enjoyment of the benefits that could be derived therefrom.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  direct  the  Local  Disaster Risk Management Officer and Municipal Budget Officer to properly charge the disbursements  made  against  LDRRMF  in  accordance  with  DBM-DILG  Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated  March  25,  2013  and  other  related guidelines on the  utilization  of  the  LDRRM  Fund,  and  prioritize  the projects and programs  which  address  disaster  prevention,  preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.

We recommended therefore, that the Local Chief Executive direct  the Local Disaster Risk Management Officer to prepare and submit the annual  LDRRMFIP according to the guidelines embodied in COA 2012-002  dated  September  12,  2012.

Moreover, we recommended that the LDRRMFIP be prepared as  to  include  the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF of the previous years so that  funds will be appropriately monitored and ensure its proper use.

We recommended that the Municipality reorganize its Solid  Waste  Management Board to include the Chairperson of the Sangguniang Kabataan Federation  and a representative of the manufacturing or packaging industry to conform  with the membership requirement under RA 9003 and to optimize the involvement  of youth and other industry sectors in solid  waste  management  activities  of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Solid Waste Management  Board  review  and update its 10-Year Solid  Waste  Management  Plan  in  accordance  with  the provision  of  RA  9003  to  ensure  the   viability,   sustainability   and effectiveness of all programs, projects and activities included therein.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive  thru  the  Sangguniang  Bayan issue and enforce ordinances  related  to  the  establishment  of  Materials Recovery Facility in every barangay or cluster of  barangays  in  compliance with Section 32 of RA 9003 to effect the proper segregation, collection  and disposal of solid wastes.

We recommended that  the  Solid  Waste  Management  Board  prepare  its  own monitoring  of  the  status  of  implementation  and  utilization  of  funds appropriated for  solid  waste  management  programs/projects/activities  as embodied in the annual budget.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive  instruct  the  Municipal Budget Officer to implement proper identification of the  AIP  Code  in  the recording      of      obligations      against      appropriations      per programs/projects/activities  to  enable   proper   monitoring   of   budget utilization and to effectively evaluate  the  extent  of  implementation  of various programs and projects of the municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive take the following courses  of action:

     a. Direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to create a certified  master list of all stallholders in the Pavia Public Market, and prepare  a report for  each  lessee  stating  the  monthly  rentals  expected, rentals actually paid, and rental delinquencies, if  any,  so  that collections are properly monitored and  appropriate  penalties  are imposed.


     b. Direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to develop plans and programs to  enhance  the  local  revenue  generation  capability   of   the municipality.

We  recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the   concerned officials and employees to take  immediate  action  on  the  matters  listed below:

      a.    Comply with the  schedule  of  GAD  planning  and  budgeting  by submitting the GPB  to  the  PPDO  for  review  within  the  period prescribed in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC 2016-01.


      b.    Be mindful of  the  gender  issues  or  gender  mandate  of  the municipality and carefully identify the activities that can address GAD issues and concerns in the locality.
The recommendations for each prior year finding are included in the respective sub-items (30.1 to 30.11) above and are reiterated for implementation as stated.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned continue to exert efforts to collect outstanding cash advances and unsettled receivables. Moreover, guidelines and procedures on the request for write-off of unliquidated cash advances must be adhered to should management decide to file a request.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC Municipal Accountant that stale checks amounting to P212,294.56  in the Bank Reconciliation Statements that are long outstanding be given attention and be adjusted in the books with their complete supporting documents to present a more reliable Cash in Bank balance in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant and officials concerned to ensure that financial assistance / fund transfers to other LGUs for the implementation of certain programs and projects of the municipality be covered by an Approved POW (for infrastructure projects) or Approved Project Expenditures, and other documentary requirements enumerated in Sections 3.1.1 and 3.1.3 of COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant make sure that fund transfers be recorded in the Due from LGUs account instead of being outright expensed so that the municipality can monitor the implementation of the proposed projects and programs.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Local School Board immediately stop the utilization of SEF for purposes other than those provided in RA 5447 and RA 7160 and in harmony with DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned allocate in its budget 1% of its IRA to fund the implementation of programs of the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC).

We also recommended that the municipality’s LCPC prepare the Annual Work and Financial Plan, Local Development Plan for Children and the Local Investment Plan for Children to serve as guide for an effective implementation of its programs/projects/activities. It should also be included in the Annual Investment Program of the municipality to ensure its funding.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

b. c. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned prioritize only programs/projects/activities which are feasible in its 20% MDF and to make sure that such are attainable within the time frame set. 

We also recommended that there should be proper monitoring of the progress of the project implementation so as to ensure that it is accomplished in a timely, efficient and effective manner.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Engineer to prepare and submit the Inspection Report as basis for the preparation of the Certificate of Completion before final payment is made to the contractor.

We also recommended that the municipality include in the pakyaw contracts information as to the date the contract work for the project should commence and the duration of the project completion to enable proper verification of the project implementation.

We further recommended that for projects wherein completion time was extended, requests for time extension and notice approval thereof should be attached to the disbursement vouchers to allow proper review thereof. 

Furthermore, we recommended that the municipality closely monitor the timeliness of the execution of contracts on infrastructure projects to avoid unnecessary delays and to demand for payment of liquidated damages if warranted.

We recommended that Local Chief Executive and the LDRRMO formulate feasible plans for PPAs to be included in the municipality’s annual LDRRMPs and LDRRMIPs to maximize the utilization of the allocation for LDRRM Program, Projects and Activities. 

We likewise recommended that only priority projects be included in the DRRM programs that are congruent to LDRRM guidelines and objectives. 

We also recommended that the remaining unutilized LDRRMF that have lapsed the 5 year period be reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund and shall be made available for other socials services.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Solid Waste Management Board prepare a comprehensive workplan for SWM operation indicating all activities needed to efficiently and effectively perform SWM functions during the year, with corresponding budget and fund source per activity, timetable, implementing office and strategies to implement said activities.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive provide for the establishment of its MRF and issue ordinances to enforce the mandatory segregation of solid wastes among its component barangays. 

We likewise recommended that the SWMB enact and enforce laws and ordinances relative to the implementation of activities under the SWM Plan and establish proper management and monitoring mechanisms to enable its efficient and effective implementation. 

We finally recommended that the municipality provide for adequate financial resources to effect the above mentioned recommendations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive spearhead the creation of GAD Monitoring and Evaluation Team to facilitate the proper monitoring and evaluation of the implementation of GAD activities in the municipality.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to return to the source agency all unused/excess balance of fund transfer after the implementation of the project for maximum utilization of fund to finance programs and projects for the benefit of the general public.

We recommend the refund of the excess amount of Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2013 totaling ₱3,559,500.00 paid to the municipal officials and employees.

We recommended that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  and  officials  concerned
ensure to:


a.    Establish the existence,  condition,  validity  and  accuracy  of  its
   fixed assets by conducting a physical inventory at least once a  year  in
   compliance with Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume I  and
   Section 156 of COA Circular No. 92-386 which  prescribes  the  Rules  and
   Regulations on Supply and Property Management in the LGUs;

b.     Expedite  the  inventory-taking  in  accordance  with  the  following
   prescribed procedures:

      Creation of Inventory Committee

   A committee shall be formed to take charge  of  the  physical  inventory-
   taking, consisting of the representative of the local chief executive  as
   chairman, the  general  services  officer,  and  the  supply  accountable
   officer of the department or office concerned.
      Property Identification and Coding

      Government Property shall be properly labeled and  identified  by  the
   Inventory Committee. Inventory labels of special materials shall  contain
   among others the individual property number to be  provided  by  the  LGU
   concerned. These numbers shall be uniformly  printed  in  India  ink,  to
   achieve permanence and best results, and shall be posted on prominent but
   secure portion of the property for easy identification.

      Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE)

      An officer or employee who receives and is in possession  or  physical
   control of the property shall be issued an ARE. Such ARE shall be renewed
   every three (3) years or every time there is a change  in  accountability
   as required by NGAS, Volume II.

      Inventory Reports

      The report on the physical count  of  property,  plant  and  equipment
   shall be prepared on the format prescribed by the NGAS,  Volume  II.  The
   report shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned not later than January
   31 of each year for the property, plant and equipment.

   records.

implementation  of  its  programs/projects/activities.  It  should  also  be
included in the Annual Investment Program of the municipality to ensure  its
funding.

We also recommended  that  the  council  formulate  and/or  update  existing
policies on child rights and to outline the implementation procedures so  as
to enable its effective and efficient execution.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant maintain a special account for the 20% MDF and prepare the required financial statements as enumerated in the above mentioned guidelines in order to provide a comprehensive information as to the extent of implementation of programs/projects undertaken by the municipality that was sourced from its development fund.

We recommend that the municipality comply with the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 on the utilization of the 20% MDF and discontinue charging expenditures that does not partake the nature of investment or capital outlay in its development fund. Moreover, concerned officials shall ensure that projects paid out of the development fund and taken up in the books as capital outlays are indeed capital expenditure in nature to avoid overstatement of assets and understatement of expenses in the financial statements.

We recommended that the municipality discontinue the practice of disbursing funds through reimbursement basis for the payment of financial assistance to students; instead, checks drawn shall be made payable directly to the creditor to whom the money is due or in certain instances when it may be very difficult, impractical or impossible to make payments by check, payments may be made by the disbursing officer in the form of cash through his cash advance in which case, it shall strictly adhere to provisions of COA Circular No 97-002 on granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the concerned PHW to refund the overpayment of hazard pay amounting to ₱17,994.56 for the CY 2016, and that the Municipal Accountant comply with the prescribed guidelines under RA 7305 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for the payment of hazard pay.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require concerned offices to set up clear implementing guidelines in carrying out the program which aims to:

1. Define the target beneficiaries and classify them (for instance indigent, vulnerable & disadvantaged, informal sector, or those who are in crisis situation based on the assessment of the Social Workers pursuant to Section 3.2.1 of DSWD MC No. 4 Series of 2015).

2. Set clear criteria in providing ceilings for the amounts of assistance per category of case/illness, and target beneficiaries.

3. Establish control measures in granting of claims (for instance avoid duplicate grants wherein beneficiaries are family members belonging to the same household and claiming for the same subject). Likewise, issuance of Certificate of Indigency will be given to indigent proponents only.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to remit all collections promptly and intact with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day pursuant to Section 32 of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units Manual, Volume I, and The General Provisions of DOF Order No. 52-96 to prevent risk of improper use or possible loss.

We also recommended that collections and deposits should be closely monitored, so as to avoid the accumulation of cash.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned persons to liquidate or refund immediately the cash advance granted for travel and other expenditures to avoid understating the expense and overstating the asset and equity accounts as well as, prevent the exposure of funds to risk of misapplication. Effort should be exerted to recover the cash advance granted or cause its liquidation/settlement by sending demand letters to the concerned persons.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary JEV to reflect the liquidation of the cash advance for confidential and intelligence fund pursuant to Item 6.2 of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.

We recommended that the Municipal GAD Focal Point System ensure that the HGDG test be administered in the preparation of its GAD Accomplishment Report and be included in the GAD AR together with the actions taken by the municipality on the COA GAD related audit findings and recommendations to enable proper evaluation of the reported accomplishments for the year.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive instruct the members of the municipality’s SWMB to closely monitor the implementation of Section 32 of RA 9003 among its component barangays to enable proper segregation of solid waste at the household level and to facilitate its efficient collection and disposal. Also, to strengthen the Information, Education and Communication (IEC) campaign of the municipality so that its constituents will be aware of the present solid waste management condition of the municipality and be able to actively participate in the present SWM programs.

We recommend that management should come up with an approved guideline for effective implementation of the program specifically on the enforcement of collection.

In addition, a contract or agreement should be instituted between the debtor and the creditor to support legal claim for receivable.

Further, the management should double its effort to collect the loan overdue balance calculated at ₱79,250.00.

We recommend that the Local Revenue Collecting Officers and deputized Barangay Treasurers remit their collection on time and intact to the Liquidating Officer and deposit the same intact within reglementary period in compliance to the above-cited provision of PD 1445 as well as Section 32 of NGASM.

Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer should strictly enforce the daily turnover of collections by the collecting officers under his control to prevent the incurrence of the above cited deficiencies.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count of PPE and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) to the Office of the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year.  In the absence of the General Services Office, the Treasurer’s Office and the Accounting Office each assign personnel to maintain separate ledger cards for all property and to work on the reconciliation of the accounting and property records with the Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment in order to establish correctness of the PPE accounts.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned Municipal Officials stop the practice of appropriating the 20% Development Fund for MOOE.

The Municipality should strictly adhere to DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1, dated April 13, 2011 on the appropriation and utilization of 20% of the Annual Internal Revenue Allotment for development projects.

We recommend that management should come up with an approved guideline for effective implementation of the program specifically on the enforcement of collection.

In addition, a contract or agreement should be instituted between the debtor and the creditor to support legal claim for receivable. Further, the management should double its effort to collect the loan overdue balance calculated at ₱79,250.00.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned department heads to submit the duly accomplished AAPSI on each findings and recommendations, as presented in Annex I, and discussed in detail in Part II – Detailed Findings and Recommendations portion of the CY 2014 Annual Audit Report, in compliance with Section 88 of the General Provisions of RA No. 10633, otherwise known as the General Appropriations Act of 2014.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require all concerned officers and employees to liquidate their long outstanding cash advances and strictly adhere to the aforementioned provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and pertinent provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 and COA Circular No. 2012-004 to ensure proper granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We also recommend the immediate refund of all unliquidated cash advances, otherwise, withhold the salaries of all officers and employees with outstanding cash advances until the amounts involved are fully restituted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to return to the source agency all unused/excess balance of fund transfer after the implementation of the project for maximum utilization of fund to finance programs and projects for the benefit of the general public.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive stop the practice of charging expenditures not within the purview of RA 5447.  They should see to it that activities to be funded by the SEF are included in the list of projects/activities that could be charged against the Special Education Fund pursuant to the pertinent provisions of Republic Act No. 5447.

We recommend the refund of the excess amount of Productivity Enhancement Incentive for CY 2013 totalling ₱3,559,500.00 paid to the municipal officials and employees.

We also recommend that municipal officials see to it that payments for allowances and other personnel benefits should be based on laws, rules and regulations authorizing such payments to avoid the suspension and disallowance of the amount.

We recommend the refund of the PEI amounting to P 4,446,000.00 in excess of its PS limitation granted to its employees since it is contrary to the provisions of Section 325 (a) of RA 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.

We also recommend that the LGU management initiate and optimize the generation of resources and revenues, and ensure that all taxes and other revenues of the LGU are collected in order to increase the allowable PS level.  Pertinent thereto, the Sangguniang Bayan should enact the corresponding ordinances levying taxes, fees and charges and prescribe the rates thereof and should come up with judicious and holistic evaluation of the reasonableness of projected revenues and other receipts.

We recommend that the LPDO spearhead the setting up and maintenance of the GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation with the assistance of LGU GFPS, DILG Operations Field Officer and other data-producing LGU departments and national government agencies operating at the local level.

The Management should also follow the requirements and timetable set for the submission GAD PB and AR. HGDG test should be administered as basis in determining actual expenditure that can be attributed to the GAD budget. The Municipality should also have a GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) Executive Committee (Execom), a Technical Working Group (TWG), and/or a Secretariat. The LCE should issue an Executive Order creating the Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) Team who will submit a GAD Evaluation Report to the DILG Regional Office at the end of the LCE’s three-year term.

We recommend that officials concerned submit the documents required under Section 3.1 of the Revised Guidelines and Documentary Requirements for Common Government Transactions prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001, dated June 14, 2012, to establish the validity of fund transfer.

Furthermore, the municipality is enjoined to always comply with the pertinent laws, government rules and regulations in the disbursement of public funds to avoid suspension and disallowance.

It is recommended that the disbursement vouchers and their relevant documents that should have supported the checks issued be submitted.  If locating these vouchers proves to be futile, the concerned finance officials should reconstruct these documents.

Otherwise, we recommended for the suspension of payment of salaries of concerned employees and officials.

We recommend that the Local revenue Collecting Officers and deputized Barangay Treasurers remit their collection on time and intact to the Liquidating Officer and deposit the same intact within reglementary period in compliance to the above-cited provision of P.D. 1445 as well as Section 32 of NGASM.

Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer should strictly enforce the daily turnover of collections by the collecting officers under his control to prevent the incurrence of the above cited deficiencies.
